items.q: What does a 'large scale' map mean?
items.correct: A large-scale map covers a small area in high detail (e.g. 1:25,000 OS map of one town); a small-scale map covers a large area in less detail (e.g. 1:1,000,000 world atlas).
items.misconception: A large-scale map shows a large area.
items.random_wrong: Large scale means the map is physically big.
items.why: This is counter-intuitive but mathematically logical: 1/25,000 is a larger fraction than 1/1,000,000. Students reverse the terminology and consistently mark themselves down on map skill questions.

items.q: What are the three cells of global atmospheric circulation?
items.correct: The Hadley cell (equator to 30 degrees), Ferrel cell (30 to 60 degrees) and Polar cell (60 degrees to poles) together redistribute heat from equator to poles.
items.misconception: There is one big Hadley cell from the equator to each pole.
items.random_wrong: The three cells are tropical, temperate and arctic, named after climate zones.
items.why: Students often learn 'Hadley' as the only cell. There are three in each hemisphere, and their boundaries (at 30 and 60 degrees) explain global pressure belts, deserts and the polar front.

items.q: Why does oceanic crust subduct beneath continental crust?
items.correct: Oceanic crust is denser (basaltic) than continental crust (granitic), so when they collide the denser oceanic plate sinks into the mantle, forming a trench and volcanic arc.
items.misconception: Oceanic crust subducts because the ocean pushes it down with its weight.
items.random_wrong: Continental crust subducts because it is thicker and heavier.
items.why: Density, not water pressure, drives subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, which is why continent-continent collisions produce fold mountains instead of trenches.
Correct answer: Hess. “Wegener” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the Tohoku earthquake magnitude?
items.why: It's 9.0. “7.0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How tall (m) was the Tohoku tsunami?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 m. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
